learningGoal: What happens when you squash two words together into a contraction (a shortened word like don't or I'm) — and why everyone does it!
The secret the apostrophe (the ' mark) is hiding — it marks the exact spot where letters disappeared

screens.title: Where did the missing letters go?
screens.body:
Aisha is writing a letter to her pen pal in Scotland and wrote:

**"I'm really excited to visit Edinburgh this summer."**

See that little floating comma? That's an **apostrophe** — and it's hiding a secret! **I'm** is actually **I am** squashed together. The apostrophe marks the spot where the missing letters used to be!

visualProps.text: I'm really excited to visit Edinburgh this summer.
visualProps.label: Spot the contraction:

screens.title: The squash-and-mark trick!
screens.body: Think of contractions like squashing two words together and leaving a little marker where you removed the letters. It's like a treasure map — the apostrophe says "letters were buried here!"

interaction.correctFeedback: Nailed it! Squash, remove, mark — you've got the recipe! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Remember: start with the two full words, then squash them together.

steps.text: Start with two words: "do not"
steps.why: These are the full, boring, long versions!

steps.text: Squash them together and remove letters: drop the "o"
steps.why: We're making it snappier — like how you'd actually say it

steps.text: Stick an apostrophe where the letter was: don't
steps.why: The ' is like a flag saying "a letter used to live here!"

steps.text: I am → I'm
steps.why: The "a" vanished and ' took its place!

screens.title: Now you try!
screens.body: Time to squash some words! **"do not"** — what does it become when you contract it?

interaction.question: What is the contraction of "do not"?
options: don't
donot
do'not
do-not
do not
interaction.correctAnswer: don't
interaction.correctFeedback: You've got it! "do not" squashes down to **don't** — the "o" disappeared and the apostrophe took its place! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: So close! "do not" becomes **don't**. The apostrophe sits right where the "o" used to be.

visualProps.text: I don't want to miss the castle!
visualProps.label: Check the punctuation:

screens.title: Contractions — you've got this!
screens.body: Here's everything you need to remember:

steps.text: A contraction squashes two words into one
steps.why: It's how people actually talk — quicker and more natural!

steps.text: The apostrophe marks the spot where letters vanished
steps.why: Think of it as a little flag: "letters were here!"

steps.text: Learn the big ones: don't, can't, it's, I'm, they're, we've
steps.why: You'll see these in almost every exam paper — knowing them is like a superpower!

steps.text: Secret check: expand it back into two words
steps.why: If 'don't' turns back into 'do not', you nailed it! ✓

learningGoal: You'll become an apostrophe detective — spotting missing or wrongly placed apostrophes (the ' mark) in contractions (shortened words)
The sneaky mistakes that trip people up with contractions — and how to dodge them

screens.title: Spot the sneaky mistake!
screens.body:
Oliver is checking his friend's homework — but one contraction has gone wrong! Can you find it?

**"I cant wait for the school trip to London!"**

visualProps.text: I cant wait for the school trip to London!
visualProps.label: Spot the punctuation issue:

screens.title: Here's what went wrong!
screens.body: Oops! Oliver forgot the apostrophe in 'can't'. This is one of the most common mistakes — even grown-ups get it wrong sometimes!

steps.text: ✗ "I cant wait for the school trip to London!"
steps.why: Oliver forgot the apostrophe in 'can't'

steps.text: ✓ "I can't wait for the school trip to London!"
steps.why: can't = can not — the apostrophe replaces the missing 'o'

steps.text: Golden rule: the apostrophe goes EXACTLY where the letters were removed
steps.why: Not before, not after — right in the spot where the letters used to live!

screens.title: Fix this sentence!
screens.body:
Which version is correct?

**"We do'nt need our coats today."**

interaction.question: Which is the correct version?
options: We don't need our coats today.
We do'nt need our coats today.
We dont need our coats today.
We don`t need our coats today.
We don,t need our coats today.
interaction.correctAnswer: We don't need our coats today.
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! The apostrophe goes where the 'o' in 'not' was removed — don't, NOT do'nt ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct version is: **"We don't need our coats today."**. The apostrophe goes where the 'o' in 'not' was removed — don't, NOT do'nt

visualProps.text: We do'nt need our coats today.

screens.title: Avoid these contraction mistakes
screens.body: When you proofread, always check your contractions!

steps.text: Never forget the apostrophe: cant → can't
steps.why: Without it, 'cant' is a completely different word!

steps.text: Put the apostrophe where the letter was removed
steps.why: does not → doesn't (NOT does'nt)

steps.text: Use an apostrophe, not a comma: we're (NOT we,re)
steps.why: Commas and apostrophes look similar but do very different jobs

steps.text: Quick check: can you expand it back to two words?
steps.why: If can't = can not, you've placed it correctly ✓

learningGoal: You'll discover how one tiny apostrophe (the ' mark) can show that something belongs to someone
The trick to knowing where the apostrophe goes when one person owns something versus when lots of people do — singular and plural possession (ownership)

screens.title: Who does it belong to?
screens.body:
Ella is labelling things at school. Ella needs to show that things **belong** to someone.

For example: **the dog's bone** means the bone belonging to the dog.

But here's the tricky bit — where does the apostrophe go? It depends on whether there's ONE owner or MORE THAN ONE!

visualProps.text: the dog's bone — the dogs' kennel
visualProps.label: One owner vs many:

screens.title: Three rules — and they're easier than you think!
screens.body: We saw **the dog's bone** and **the dogs' kennel** — notice the apostrophe (the ' mark) is in a different place! Once you learn these three rules, you'll nail possessive apostrophes every time:

steps.text: Singular (one owner): add 's → the dog's bone
steps.why: One dog, so apostrophe then s — easy!

steps.text: Plural ending in s: add just ' → the dogs' kennel
steps.why: More than one dog — the s is already there, so just pop the apostrophe after it

steps.text: Irregular plural (no s): add 's → the children's playground
steps.why: "children" doesn't end in s, so treat it like singular — sneaky!

steps.text: NEVER put an apostrophe on pronouns: its, yours, hers, theirs
steps.why: Pronouns already show ownership — they don't need any help!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What does **"the cat's toy"** mean?

interaction.question: What does "the cat's toy" mean?
options: the toy belonging to one cat
the toy belonging to many cat
More than one cat
The toy is a cat
It is a contraction (two words joined)
interaction.correctAnswer: the toy belonging to one cat
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! **"the cat's toy"** means the toy belonging to one cat. You've cracked the code! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: So close! **"the cat's toy"** means **the toy belonging to one cat**. When you see 's on a singular noun, it means belonging to — you'll get it next time!

visualProps.text: the cat's toy
visualProps.label: What does this mean?

screens.title: Possessive apostrophes — you've got this!
screens.body: Here's your recipe for showing ownership — follow it and you'll never go wrong:

steps.text: One owner → add 's: the dog's bone
steps.why: Singular nouns always get apostrophe + s — the easiest rule!

steps.text: More than one (ends in s) → add ': the dogs' bones
steps.why: The s is already there doing its job, so just pop the apostrophe after it

steps.text: Irregular plurals → add 's: the children's toys
steps.why: Children doesn't end in s, so treat it like singular — this catches people out!

steps.text: NEVER on pronouns: its, yours, hers, ours, theirs
steps.why: These already mean 'belonging to' — no apostrophe needed. That's a superpower for the exam! ✓

learningGoal: You'll sharpen your eye for possessive (ownership) apostrophes that have wandered to the wrong spot
A surprising rule — why pronouns (words like he, she, it, they) never need an apostrophe to show ownership

screens.title: Apostrophe alert — something's wrong!
screens.body:
Leo is marking his younger sister's writing. Something is wrong here — can you be the detective?

**"The teacher's all agreed the trips' destination was perfect."**

Apostrophes have snuck into the wrong places!

visualProps.text: The teacher's all agreed the trips' destination was perfect.
visualProps.label: Spot the punctuation issue:

screens.title: Here's where it all went wrong!
screens.body:
Leo put an apostrophe on 'teachers' (no possession) and on 'trips' (wrong place).

Don't worry — this is one of the most common mistakes in English, and even adults get it wrong!

**Rule:** 'teachers' here is just a plural (more than one teacher) — no apostrophe needed. 'Trip's' shows the destination belongs to the trip.

steps.text: WRONG: "The teacher's all agreed the trips' destination was perfect."
steps.why: put an apostrophe on 'teachers' (no possession) and on 'trips' (wrong place)

steps.text: RIGHT: "The teachers all agreed the trip's destination was perfect."
steps.why: 'teachers' here is just a plural (more than one teacher) — no apostrophe needed. 'Trip's' shows the destination belongs to the trip

steps.text: Ask yourself: does something BELONG to this word?
steps.why: If yes → apostrophe. If it's just 'more than one' → no apostrophe. Simple!

screens.title: Your turn to fix it!
screens.body:
Which version is correct?

**"The dog wagged it's tail happily."**

interaction.question: Which is the correct version?
options: The dog wagged its tail happily.
The dog wagged it's tail happily.
The dog wagged it's tail happily!
The dog wagged it's tail happily!
The dog wagged it's tail happily!
interaction.correctAnswer: The dog wagged its tail happily.
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'its' (no apostrophe) means 'belonging to it'. 'it's' with an apostrophe means 'it is' or 'it has' ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct version is: **"The dog wagged its tail happily."**. 'its' (no apostrophe) means 'belonging to it'. 'it's' with an apostrophe means 'it is' or 'it has'

visualProps.text: The dog wagged it's tail happily.
visualProps.label: Fix the punctuation:

screens.title: Possessive apostrophes — nailed it!
screens.body: Before you hand in any writing, run through this checklist. It only takes a few seconds and it'll make your work shine:

steps.text: Does something BELONG to this word? If not, no apostrophe
steps.why: Plain plurals (cats, dogs, books) never need an apostrophe — don't let them trick you!

steps.text: One owner ending in s? Add 's: the boss's office
steps.why: Yes, even when the word already ends in s — that surprises a lot of people!

steps.text: More than one owner ending in s? Add just ': the dogs' leads
steps.why: The s is already there doing its job

steps.text: Never put apostrophes on pronouns: its, yours, theirs
steps.why: Pronouns already show ownership — getting this right makes examiners go 'wow'! ✓

learningGoal: You'll learn the real rules for when a word deserves a capital letter — it's not as many as you'd think!
The words that look like they should have capitals but actually don't — and how to tell the difference

screens.title: Did you know some words DEMAND a capital?
screens.body:
Daisy is writing a postcard from her holiday in Cornwall. Daisy wrote: **"The beach in Cornwall was amazing."**

The first word has a capital letter — but which OTHER words need capitals? Some words are divas and insist on being capitalised! Let's find out which ones.

visualProps.text: The beach in Cornwall was amazing.
visualProps.label: Which words need capitals?

screens.title: Four rules that cover (almost) everything!
screens.body: In Daisy's sentence, **"Cornwall"** needs a capital because it's a a place. Good news — there are only four rules to learn, and you probably know most of them already:

interaction.sentence: Days and months need capitals, but ____ do not
options: seasons
proper nouns
names
places
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Seasons (spring, summer, autumn, winter) don't need capitals — knowing that is a superpower for the exam! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Seasons like spring and summer don't need capitals, unlike days and months. This one trips up loads of people!

steps.text: Rule 1: The first word of every sentence
steps.why: "The beach in Cornwall was amazing." — the first word always gets a capital. The easiest one!

steps.text: Rule 2: Names of people, places and things (proper nouns)
steps.why: Cornwall is a place — proper nouns always get the VIP treatment

steps.text: Rule 3: Days of the week and months of the year
steps.why: on Saturday in July — both get capitals (they're special enough to earn them!)

steps.text: Rule 4: The word 'I' is always a capital
steps.why: Even in the middle of a sentence: 'Then I went home' — because you're important!

steps.text: NOT seasons: summer has no capital
steps.why: Here's the sneaky one — seasons are common nouns, not proper nouns. This catches loads of people out!

screens.title: Fix the capitals!
screens.body:
This sentence needs fixing:

**"last march, we visited the tower of london on a tuesday."**

interaction.question: Which is the correctly capitalised version?
options: Last March, we visited the Tower of London on a Tuesday.
last march, we visited the tower of london on a tuesday.
last March, we visited the Tower of London on a Tuesday.
LAST MARCH, WE VISITED THE TOWER OF LONDON ON A TUESDAY.
Last march, we visited the tower of london on a tuesday.
interaction.correctAnswer: Last March, we visited the Tower of London on a Tuesday.
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! You spotted every single one: Last (sentence start), March (month), Tower of London (proper noun), Tuesday (day). ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: So close! The correct version is: **"Last March, we visited the Tower of London on a Tuesday."**. Capitals needed: Last (sentence start), March (month), Tower of London (proper noun), Tuesday (day). You'll nail it next time!

visualProps.text: last march, we visited the tower of london on a tuesday.
visualProps.label: Fix the punctuation:

screens.title: Capitals — you've cracked the code!
screens.body: Remember: capitals are for special words, not just any word you feel like! Here's your cheat sheet:

steps.text: Start of a sentence: always a capital
steps.why: The easiest rule — you've been doing this for years!

steps.text: Proper nouns: names of people, places, titles
steps.why: London, Mrs Smith, River Thames — they're important enough to earn a big letter

steps.text: Days and months: Monday, January
steps.why: But NOT seasons: spring, summer, autumn, winter — remember this exam trick!

steps.text: The word 'I': always a capital letter
steps.why: Because you're important — you deserve a big letter! ✓

learningGoal: You'll become a capital letter detective — catching ones that are missing AND ones that shouldn't be there
Why 'summer' doesn't get a capital but 'Monday' does — the surprising rules that catch people out

screens.title: Capital letter chaos — can you fix it?
screens.body:
Ethan is checking his geography homework. This sentence is a mess — capitals everywhere they shouldn't be, and missing where they should!

**"in Autumn, we visited the lake district with our Teacher."**

Can you spot what's gone wrong?

visualProps.text: in Autumn, we visited the lake district with our Teacher.
visualProps.label: Spot the punctuation issue:

screens.title: Here's where it went wrong!
screens.body:
Ethan capitalised 'Autumn' (a season) and 'Teacher' (common noun) but forgot to capitalise 'In' (sentence start) and 'Lake District' (proper noun).

This is really common — even grown-ups muddle up which words deserve capitals!

**Rule:** Seasons and common nouns like 'teacher' don't need capitals, but proper nouns and sentence starts do.

steps.text: WRONG: "in Autumn, we visited the lake district with our Teacher."
steps.why: capitalised 'Autumn' (a season) and 'Teacher' (common noun) but forgot to capitalise 'In' (sentence start) and 'Lake District' (proper noun)

steps.text: RIGHT: "In autumn, we visited the Lake District with our teacher."
steps.why: Seasons and common nouns like 'teacher' don't need capitals, but proper nouns and sentence starts do

steps.text: Ask: is this a proper noun (name of a specific thing)?
steps.why: If yes → capital. If it's a general word → no capital. That one question solves almost everything!

screens.title: Now you try!
screens.body:
Which version has the capitals in all the right places?

**"my Family went to paris in Summer to see the eiffel tower."**

interaction.question: Which is correctly capitalised?
options: My family went to Paris in summer to see the Eiffel Tower.
my Family went to paris in Summer to see the eiffel tower.
My Family went to paris in Summer to see the eiffel tower.
MY FAMILY WENT TO PARIS IN SUMMER TO SEE THE EIFFEL TOWER.
my family went to paris in summer to see the eiffel tower.
interaction.correctAnswer: My family went to Paris in summer to see the Eiffel Tower.
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'My' (sentence start), 'Paris' and 'Eiffel Tower' (proper nouns) need capitals. 'family' and 'summer' don't. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct version is: **"My family went to Paris in summer to see the Eiffel Tower."**. 'My' (sentence start), 'Paris' and 'Eiffel Tower' (proper nouns) need capitals. 'family' and 'summer' don't.

screens.title: Capital letters — sorted!
screens.body: Before you hand in any writing, run through this quick checklist. It takes ten seconds and could save you marks:

steps.text: Does the sentence start with a capital? If not, add one
steps.why: The most basic rule — you'd be surprised how often people miss it!

steps.text: Are all proper nouns capitalised? (names, places, titles)
steps.why: London, Mrs Smith, the River Thames — they deserve the big letter treatment

steps.text: Are days and months capitalised?
steps.why: Monday, January — but NOT spring or summer (remember the trick!)

steps.text: Have you wrongly capitalised any common nouns?
steps.why: Words like 'teacher', 'school', 'assembly' don't need capitals — catching this makes your writing look really polished ✓

learningGoal: You'll discover why commas are like the gaps between items on a shelf — they keep everything in a list neat and clear
The big question: do you put a comma before 'and' or 'or' at the end of a list? You'll find out!

screens.title: Imagine reading a list with no commas...
screens.body:
Kai is writing a shopping list for his mum's birthday party. Kai needs to write a list of items in a sentence.

Without commas: **"balloons streamers cake and candles"** — yikes, that's a jumbled mess!

With commas: **"balloons, streamers, cake and candles"** — ahh, much better! Commas are like little signposts between each item.

visualProps.text: balloons, streamers, cake and candles
visualProps.label: Items separated by commas:

screens.title: The simplest comma rule there is!
screens.body: Kai's list **"balloons, streamers, cake and candles"** uses commas to separate the items. The rule is beautifully simple: put a **comma** after every item in a list, except the last one (which usually has **'and'** or **'or'** before it).

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! You've got the recipe down! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Start by writing the first item, then add commas between each one. You'll get it!

steps.text: Write the first item: "balloons"
steps.why: No comma needed yet — just get started!

steps.text: Add a comma, then the next item: "balloons, streamers"
steps.why: The comma tells your reader 'here comes another one!'

steps.text: Keep going: "balloons, streamers, cake"
steps.why: A comma after every item except the last — you're getting the hang of it!

steps.text: Add 'and' before the last item: "balloons, streamers, cake and candles"
steps.why: Use 'and' (or 'or') instead of a comma before the final item — like a finishing flourish

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which sentence has the commas in the right places?

interaction.question: Which list is punctuated correctly?
options: apples, bananas, grapes, oranges and pears
apples bananas, grapes oranges, and pears
apples bananas grapes oranges pears
apples, bananas, grapes, oranges, pears
apples and bananas and grapes and oranges and pears
interaction.correctAnswer: apples, bananas, grapes, oranges and pears
interaction.correctFeedback: Nailed it! Commas between each item, 'and' before the last one — you make it look easy! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: So close! The correct version is: **"apples, bananas, grapes, oranges and pears"**. Put a comma after every item except the last, where you use 'and'. You'll get it next time!

visualProps.text: apples  /  bananas  /  grapes  /  oranges  /  pears
visualProps.label: Put these into a sentence with commas:

screens.title: List commas — easy as 1, 2, 3!
screens.body: You've got this! Here's how to punctuate a list every single time:

steps.text: Put a comma after each item in the list
steps.why: Think of commas as little separators keeping everything tidy

steps.text: Use 'and' or 'or' before the last item
steps.why: This is the finishing touch that tells your reader the list is done

steps.text: No comma before 'and' in standard British English
steps.why: Write: apples, oranges and bananas (NOT apples, oranges, and bananas)

steps.text: Read it aloud — pause where the commas are
steps.why: Your ear is actually a brilliant comma detector! If it sounds natural, you've nailed it ✓

learningGoal: You'll hunt for missing and extra commas in lists — like a proofreading pro
Why leaving out a comma can make your sentence say something completely different from what you meant!

screens.title: This list is a comma disaster!
screens.body:
Maya is checking her recipe write-up in food technology. This list has gone wrong — the commas are all over the place!

**"You will need flour sugar, eggs butter, and milk."**

Can you spot what needs fixing?

visualProps.text: You will need flour sugar, eggs butter, and milk.
visualProps.label: Spot the punctuation issue:

screens.title: Here's what went wrong!
screens.body:
Maya missed commas after 'flour' and 'eggs', making it unclear where each item starts and ends. Don't worry — comma mistakes in lists are super common!

**Rule:** Every item in a list needs a comma after it (except the last, which gets 'and').

steps.text: WRONG: "You will need flour sugar, eggs butter, and milk."
steps.why: missed commas after 'flour' and 'eggs', making it unclear where each item starts and ends

steps.text: RIGHT: "You will need flour, sugar, eggs, butter and milk."
steps.why: Every item in a list needs a comma after it (except the last, which gets 'and')

steps.text: Top tip: read the list aloud and pause at each comma
steps.why: If it sounds choppy or confusing, your commas need fixing!

For the trip, bring a coat, a hat, gloves, and, a scarf.

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! The correct version is: **"For the trip, bring a coat, a hat, gloves and a scarf."**. Each item needs a comma after it. Don't put a comma after 'and' — 'and' replaces the final comma

screens.title: List commas — you've got this!
screens.body: Quick check before you hand in your writing — run every list through this:

steps.text: Every item needs a comma after it
steps.why: Keeps each item separate and clear — no jumbled mess!

steps.text: The last item gets 'and' (or 'or') instead
steps.why: This is how your reader knows the list is wrapping up

steps.text: Never put a comma AFTER 'and'
steps.why: 'and' replaces the comma — you don't need both. This catches loads of people out!

steps.text: Read it aloud to check it sounds right
steps.why: Your ear is actually brilliant at spotting dodgy commas! ✓

learningGoal: You'll learn the golden rule: when a clause (a group of words with a verb) kicks off your sentence, pop a comma after it
How commas team up with connectives (linking words) like 'However' and 'Meanwhile' to glue your ideas together smoothly

screens.title: One sentence has a comma, one doesn't — why?
screens.body:
Freya is writing a story about a brave knight. Here's a puzzle — look at these two sentences:

1. **"Although the dragon was enormous, the knight stood her ground."**
2. **"The knight stood her ground although the dragon was enormous."**

Sentence 1 has a comma. Sentence 2 doesn't. Same words, different order. So what's going on?

visualProps.text: Although the dragon was enormous, the knight stood her ground.
visualProps.label: Spot the clause:

screens.title: The 'extra bit first' rule!
screens.body: In Freya's first sentence, the **subordinate clause** (think of it as a bonus fact that can't stand alone) **"Although the dragon was enormous"** comes BEFORE the main clause (the main part that makes sense on its own). When the bonus bit comes first, pop a **comma** between them.

interaction.sentence: When a subordinate clause comes ____ the main clause, you need a comma between them
options: before
after
inside
without
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Bonus bit first, then a comma, then the main clause. You've got it! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! When the subordinate clause comes BEFORE the main clause, you need a comma between them. Think: extra bit first = comma needed!

steps.text: Subordinate clause first → COMMA → main clause
steps.why: "Although the dragon was enormous, the knight stood her ground." — the comma is the join!

steps.text: Main clause first → usually NO comma
steps.why: "The knight stood her ground although the dragon was enormous." flows naturally without one — nice and easy

steps.text: Connectives at the start need a comma too
steps.why: "However, the dragon was not finished yet." — always a comma after 'However'. Always!

steps.text: Look for starters: Although, Because, When, If, After, However, Meanwhile
steps.why: Spotting these words is like having a superpower — they're your comma clue!

screens.title: Add the comma!
screens.body:
This sentence needs a comma:

**"Because it was raining heavily the match was cancelled."**

interaction.question: Which version has the comma in the right place?
options: Because it was raining heavily, the match was cancelled.
Because it was raining heavily the match was cancelled.
Because, it was raining heavily the match was cancelled.
Because it was raining heavily the match was cancelled, .
interaction.correctAnswer: Because it was raining heavily, the match was cancelled.
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! The subordinate clause ('Because it was raining heavily') comes first, so you need a comma before the main clause. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct version is: **"Because it was raining heavily, the match was cancelled."**. The subordinate clause ('Because it was raining heavily') comes first, so you need a comma before the main clause.

visualProps.text: Because it was raining heavily the match was cancelled.
visualProps.label: Fix the punctuation:

screens.title: Clauses and commas — sorted!
screens.body: This rule makes your writing flow beautifully. Once you've got it, your sentences will sound really polished:

steps.text: If the subordinate clause comes FIRST → add a comma
steps.why: 'Although it rained, we still had fun.' — the comma is the bridge!

steps.text: If the main clause comes first → usually no comma
steps.why: 'We still had fun although it rained.' — it flows naturally

steps.text: Connectives at the start always get a comma
steps.why: 'However, the weather improved later.' — no exceptions!

steps.text: Starters to watch for: Although, Because, When, If, After, Since
steps.why: Spot one of these at the start and you know a comma is coming. That's exam gold! ✓

learningGoal: You'll train your eye to catch missing commas after clauses (groups of words with a verb) — the mistake almost everyone makes
The tricky spots where commas and connectives (linking words) go wrong — and how to get them right every time

screens.title: The comma's gone walkabout!
screens.body:
Archie is marking his own writing in English class. This sentence has a comma problem — it's either missing or in completely the wrong place!

**"Although he had practised for weeks he still felt nervous before the concert."**

Can you figure out where it should be?

visualProps.text: Although he had practised for weeks he still felt nervous before the concert.
visualProps.label: Spot the punctuation issue:

screens.title: Here's the fix!
screens.body:
Archie forgot the comma after the subordinate clause 'Although he had practised for weeks'. This is a really common mistake — the tricky part is figuring out where the subordinate clause ends!

**Rule:** When a subordinate clause comes first, you MUST put a comma before the main clause.

steps.text: WRONG: "Although he had practised for weeks he still felt nervous before the concert."
steps.why: forgot the comma after the subordinate clause 'Although he had practised for weeks'

steps.text: RIGHT: "Although he had practised for weeks, he still felt nervous before the concert."
steps.why: When a subordinate clause comes first, you MUST put a comma before the main clause

steps.text: Find the subordinate clause first, then put the comma at its end
steps.why: Think of the comma as a bridge between the two parts — it goes right at the join!

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct version is: **"However, the team did not give up and they scored in the last minute."**. 'However' is a connective at the start of the sentence — it always needs a comma after it.

screens.title: Comma placement — you've cracked it!
screens.body: Here's the rule one more time. Getting this right will make your writing sound really professional:

steps.text: Find the subordinate clause (the 'bonus fact' part)
steps.why: It often starts with Although, Because, When, If, After, While — these are your clue words!

steps.text: If it comes FIRST → comma at the END of that clause
steps.why: 'Although it rained, we had fun.' NOT 'Although, it rained we had fun.' — big difference!

steps.text: Connectives (However, Meanwhile, Furthermore) get a comma right after
steps.why: 'However, the plan worked.' — always, always, always a comma after the connective

steps.text: Read it aloud — pause where the comma should be
steps.why: A natural pause usually shows you exactly where the comma belongs. Trust your ear! ✓

learningGoal: You'll master how to wrap someone's exact words in speech marks — so readers can hear the characters talking
Where all the little punctuation marks go around speech — the comma, full stop, and capital letter each have their own spot

screens.title: Did you know speech has its own uniform?
screens.body:
Ruby is writing a story about two friends at the seaside. When a character speaks, you need **speech marks** (also called inverted commas) to wrap around their exact words — like a uniform that says "someone is talking!"

"Let's build a sandcastle!" shouted Ruby.

Let's learn the rules!

visualProps.text: "Let's build a sandcastle!" shouted Ruby.

screens.title: Four rules — and your dialogue will look amazing!
screens.body: Look at Ruby's dialogue: "Let's build a sandcastle!" shouted Ruby.. Follow these four rules and your dialogue will be spot-on every time:

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! You've nailed the order — open marks, capital letter, punctuation inside, comma before the reporting clause. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Start by opening the speech marks, then use a capital letter for the first spoken word. You'll get it!

steps.text: Rule 1: Speech marks go around the EXACT words spoken
steps.why: "Let's go!" — only the spoken words go inside the marks. Nothing else sneaks in!

steps.text: Rule 2: Capital letter at the start of the speech
steps.why: "Let's go!" — spoken words always start with a capital, like a mini-sentence

steps.text: Rule 3: Punctuation goes INSIDE the closing speech mark
steps.why: "Let's go!" NOT "Let's go"! — this one catches out even adults!

steps.text: Rule 4: Use a comma (not a full stop) before 'said/asked/shouted'
steps.why: "Hello," said Tom. NOT "Hello." said Tom. — the comma keeps everything connected

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct version is: **"I can see a dolphin," whispered Ella.**. Remember: speech marks around the exact words, punctuation INSIDE the closing mark.

screens.title: Speech marks — you're a pro now!
screens.body: Every time you write dialogue, follow this recipe. Getting speech marks right makes your stories look really polished:

steps.text: Open speech marks before the first spoken word
steps.why: Start with " — the door to what someone says

steps.text: Capital letter for the first word of speech
steps.why: "Hello" not "hello" — spoken words deserve the big letter treatment

steps.text: Punctuation goes INSIDE the closing speech mark
steps.why: "Hello!" not "Hello"! — this is the one examiners always check for

steps.text: Use a comma before 'said/asked/shouted'
steps.why: "Hello," said Tom — comma replaces the full stop. That's your secret weapon! ✓

learningGoal: You'll become a speech mark detective — catching errors that even adults miss in their writing

screens.title: This dialogue is full of mistakes!

Jasper forgot the comma after 'quickly', and the spoken words each need their own speech marks with punctuation inside. Don't worry — speech mark mistakes are incredibly common, but once you know what to look for, they're easy to fix!

**Rule:** Each piece of speech gets its own speech marks, and punctuation goes inside the closing mark.

steps.text: WRONG: "Come quickly" said Jasper "I found something amazing".
steps.why: forgot the comma after 'quickly', and the spoken words each need their own speech marks with punctuation inside

steps.text: RIGHT: "Come quickly," said Jasper. "I found something amazing."
steps.why: Each piece of speech gets its own speech marks, and punctuation goes inside the closing mark

steps.text: Three quick checks: capital at start, punctuation inside, comma before reporting clause

screens.title: Speech marks — three checks and you're golden!
screens.body: Before you hand in any writing with dialogue, run through these checks. They'll make your stories look really professional:

steps.text: Capital letter at the start of each piece of speech
steps.why: "Hello," not "hello," — the spoken words get the VIP treatment

steps.text: All punctuation (. , ! ?) goes INSIDE the closing speech mark
steps.why: "Stop!" not "Stop"! — this is the one that catches out grown-ups too!

steps.text: No capital letter on 'said/asked/shouted' after speech
steps.why: "Hello," said Tom (lowercase s) — the speech and reporting clause are one sentence

steps.text: New speaker = new paragraph (new line)
steps.why: This makes it crystal clear who is talking. Examiners love this! ✓

learningGoal: You'll discover when a sentence is really asking something — and needs a question mark instead of a boring full stop
When a sentence is bursting with excitement, surprise, or a command — that's when the exclamation mark gets its moment!

screens.title: Three endings, three completely different moods!
screens.body:
Evie is writing interview questions for the school newspaper. Did you know the mark at the end of a sentence completely changes its personality?

**"What is your favourite subject?"** — asking something (curious!)
**"My favourite subject is science."** — telling something (calm)
**"That is amazing!"** — strong feeling (dramatic!)

How do you pick the right one?

visualProps.text: What is your favourite subject? / My favourite subject is science. / That is amazing!
visualProps.label: Three types of sentence:

screens.title: One question that solves everything!
screens.body: We saw three sentences from Evie: a question, a statement, and an exclamation. Here's the trick — just ask yourself: **what is this sentence DOING?**

steps.text: Asking a question? → Use ?
steps.why: "What is your favourite subject?" — question words like What, Where, How are your clue!

steps.text: Telling/stating something? → Use .
steps.why: "My favourite subject is science." — just giving information, nice and calm

steps.text: Showing strong feeling? → Use !
steps.why: "That is amazing!" — surprise, excitement, anger — imagine shouting it!

steps.text: Giving a command/warning? → Use ! or .
steps.why: "Tell me more about it!" — ! for urgency ('Run!'), . for calm orders ('Please sit down.')

screens.body:

How many goals did you score.
How many goals did you score,
How many goals did you score;
How many goals did you score:
interaction.correctAnswer: How many goals did you score?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! It's a question — it asks for information, so it needs a question mark. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct answer is: **"How many goals did you score?"**. It's a question — it asks for information, so it needs a question mark.

visualProps.text: How many goals did you score
visualProps.label: Fix the punctuation:

screens.title: Sentence endings — easy as asking one question!
screens.body: Before you put that final mark down, just ask: "What is this sentence doing?" Here's your cheat sheet:

steps.text: Is it asking something? → Question mark (?)
steps.why: Look for question words: Who, What, Where, When, Why, How, Can, Do, Is — they're your clue!

steps.text: Is it telling/explaining? → Full stop (.)
steps.why: Most sentences are statements — the trusty full stop does the job

steps.text: Is it showing strong feeling? → Exclamation mark (!)
steps.why: Surprise, excitement, shock, anger, urgency — imagine shouting it!

steps.text: Never use more than one: NOT !! or ?!
steps.why: One mark is always enough in formal writing. Restraint is a superpower! ✓

learningGoal: You'll spot sentences wearing the wrong ending — like a question pretending to be a statement!
Why swapping a ? for a . (or the other way round) can totally change what a sentence means

screens.title: Wrong ending alert!

screens.title: Here's the fix!
screens.body:
Ava used a full stop instead of a question mark. This is a sneaky one — the trick is to think about what the sentence is actually doing.

**Rule:** Sentences starting with question words (What, When, How, etc.) are questions and need ?.

steps.text: WRONG: "What time does your school start."
steps.why: used a full stop instead of a question mark

steps.text: RIGHT: "What time does your school start?"
steps.why: Sentences starting with question words (What, When, How, etc.) are questions and need ?

steps.text: Ask: is the sentence ASKING something? If yes → ?
steps.why: Look for question starters: Who/What/Where/When/Why/How/Can/Do — they're your best friends here!

Help, the boat is sinking:
interaction.correctAnswer: Help, the boat is sinking!
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! This is an urgent warning/cry for help — it needs an exclamation mark to show the strong feeling. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! The correct version is: **"Help, the boat is sinking!"**. This is an urgent warning/cry for help — it needs an exclamation mark to show the strong feeling.

visualProps.text: Help, the boat is sinking.
visualProps.label: Fix the punctuation:

screens.title: Sentence endings — sorted!
screens.body: Here's a trick that works every time. Before you put the final mark, ask yourself:

steps.text: Is it a direct question? → ?
steps.why: Even dramatic or rhetorical questions use ? — they're still asking!

steps.text: Is it a statement (even if it mentions a question)? → .
steps.why: 'I wonder where it is.' is a statement about wondering — sneaky, but it's a full stop

steps.text: Is it showing strong feeling or urgency? → !
steps.why: Commands, warnings, surprise, excitement — imagine shouting it!

steps.text: When in doubt, read it aloud — your voice goes UP for a question
steps.why: If your voice rises at the end, it's a question. Your voice knows the answer! ✓

screens.title: Three punctuation marks that make you look really clever!
screens.body: Cleo is writing a fact file about British wildlife. These three punctuation marks are used less often — but using them well makes your writing stand out:

bodyParts.content: Cleo is writing a fact file about British wildlife. Look at how these three punctuation marks are used:

props.text: The red fox (Britain's most common wild predator) is found across the UK.
props.label: Brackets — extra information:

props.text: The fox eats many things: berries, mice, insects and scraps.
props.label: Colon — introduces a list:

props.text: Foxes are clever hunters; they can adapt to any habitat.
props.label: Semi-colon — joins two related ideas:

screens.title: Each one has its own special job!
screens.body: Cleo's sentences showed brackets, a colon, and a semi-colon. Think of them as tools in a toolkit — each one does something different:

steps.text: Brackets ( ): wrap around EXTRA information
steps.why: Like whispering a side note — if you removed it, the sentence would still work!

steps.text: Colon : introduces a list or explanation
steps.why: "The fox eats many things: berries, mice, insects and scraps." — the colon is like a drumroll saying 'here they come!'

steps.text: Semi-colon ; joins two RELATED complete sentences
steps.why: "Foxes are clever hunters; they can adapt to any habitat." — both halves could stand alone, but the semi-colon shows they're best friends

steps.text: No capital letter after a colon or semi-colon
steps.why: The sentence carries on — it's not starting fresh

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! You picked the perfect tool for the job — that's impressive! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: So close! The correct version is: **"You will need the following ingredients: flour, eggs, sugar and butter."**. Remember: colons introduce lists, semi-colons join related sentences, brackets add extra info. You'll get it!

screens.title: Three marks, three jobs — you've got this!
screens.body: Using these correctly makes your writing look really sophisticated. Here's your quick reference:

steps.text: Brackets ( ): extra info you could remove
steps.why: The River Thames (346 km long) flows through London. — like a whispered side note!

steps.text: Colon : introduces a list or explanation
steps.why: You need: eggs, flour and sugar. — the colon says 'here's what's coming!'

steps.text: Semi-colon ; joins two related sentences
steps.why: It was raining; we stayed inside. — two ideas, closely connected

steps.text: No capital after : or ; (the sentence continues)
steps.why: These are joiners, not sentence starters. Knowing this makes examiners impressed! ✓

learningGoal: You'll learn to spot when a colon, semi-colon, or bracket has been used in the wrong place — they get mixed up all the time!

screens.title: Wrong mark! Can you spot it?

screens.title: Here's the mix-up!

Felix used a semi-colon instead of a colon before a list. Colons and semi-colons look really similar, so mixing them up is super common!

**Rule:** Colons (:) introduce lists. Semi-colons (;) join two complete sentences.

steps.text: WRONG: "The Romans built many things; roads, bridges, baths and temples."
steps.why: used a semi-colon instead of a colon before a list

steps.text: RIGHT: "The Romans built many things: roads, bridges, baths and temples."
steps.why: Colons (:) introduce lists. Semi-colons (;) join two complete sentences

steps.text: Quick test: is it a list? → colon. Two sentences? → semi-colon

screens.title: Colons, semi-colons, brackets — nailed it!
screens.body: Remember the simple test — one question tells you which mark to use:

steps.text: Introducing a list? → Use a colon :
steps.why: The colon is like a drumroll — 'here comes the list!'

steps.text: Joining two complete sentences? → Use a semi-colon ;
steps.why: The semi-colon says 'these ideas are best friends'

steps.text: Adding removable extra info? → Use brackets ( )
steps.why: The sentence must still work without them — like a bonus side note

steps.text: No capital letter after : ; or ( )

subConcepts.name: it's vs its — The Big One

learningGoal: The it's vs its battle — you'll finally crack when the apostrophe version (it's = it is / it has) is right
When 'its' goes naked with no apostrophe — because it's showing that something belongs to 'it'
A brilliant shortcut: the 'swap in it is' trick that tells you the answer in two seconds

screens.title: The two-letter trap that catches EVERYONE!
screens.body:
Bella is writing about her pet rabbit. Did you know that **it's** and **its** cause more mistakes than almost any other words in English? Even teachers get them mixed up!

**"It's a very fluffy rabbit."** — it's = it is a very fluffy rabbit
**"The rabbit twitched its nose."** — its = the nose belonging to the rabbit

They SOUND the same but mean completely different things!

visualProps.text: "It's a very fluffy rabbit." (it is) vs "The rabbit twitched its nose." (belonging to it)
visualProps.label: it's or its?

screens.title: The magic trick that works EVERY time!
screens.body:
We saw **"It's a very fluffy rabbit."** (it's = it is a very fluffy rabbit) and **"The rabbit twitched its nose."** (its = the nose belonging to the rabbit). Here's a brilliant trick — once you learn this, you'll never get it wrong again:

Replace the word with **"it is"**. If the sentence still makes sense → use **it's**. If not → use **its**. That's it!

interaction.sentence: To check whether to use it's or its, try replacing the word with "____"
options: it is
it was
it has been
its own
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Replace with "it is" — if the sentence still makes sense, use it's. If not, use its. You've cracked the code! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! The magic trick is to replace with "it is". If the sentence works, use it's (contraction). If not, use its (possessive). Try it again!

steps.text: Test: "It's a very fluffy rabbit."
steps.why: Replace: "It is a very fluffy rabbit." — makes sense! ✓ So use it's. Easy!

steps.text: Test: "The rabbit twitched its nose."
steps.why: Replace: "The rabbit twitched it is nose" — nope, that sounds bonkers! So use its

steps.text: it's = it is / it has (a contraction — apostrophe replaces letters)
steps.why: Just like don't = do not, can't = can not — same idea!

steps.text: its = belonging to it (like his, hers, yours — NO apostrophe)
steps.why: Possessive pronouns never have apostrophes — they're special like that

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Fill in the gaps:

**"The cat licked ___ paw because ___ dirty."**

interaction.question: Which fills the gaps correctly?
options: its ... it's
it's ... its
its ... its
it's ... it's
its' ... its'
interaction.correctAnswer: its ... it's
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! 'its paw' = the paw belonging to it (possessive). 'it's dirty' = it is dirty (contraction). ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is **its ... it's**. 'its paw' = the paw belonging to it (possessive). 'it's dirty' = it is dirty (contraction). Remember: try replacing with 'it is' — if it works, use it's.

visualProps.text: The cat licked ___ paw because ___ dirty.
visualProps.label: Check the punctuation:

screens.title: it's vs its — your secret weapon!
screens.body: This is the most tested punctuation question in the 11+. Seriously! Learning this trick is like having a cheat code for the exam:

steps.text: it's = it is OR it has
steps.why: A contraction — the apostrophe replaces the missing letters, just like don't

steps.text: its = belonging to it
steps.why: A possessive pronoun — like his, hers, yours (NO apostrophe — they're special!)

steps.text: THE TRICK: replace with 'it is'
steps.why: If the sentence still makes sense → it's. If not → its. Works every single time!

steps.text: There is NO SUCH WORD as its' (with apostrophe after s)
steps.why: This form doesn't exist — it's always either it's or its. Now you know something most adults don't! ✓

learningGoal: You'll hunt down it's/its mix-ups in real sentences — the mistake that catches out even grown-ups!
You'll practise the 'swap in it is' replacement trick until it becomes second nature

screens.title: The its/it's swap — can you catch it?
screens.body:
Holly is marking her partner's work in English. These sentences have it's and its mixed up — the apostrophes have ended up in the wrong places!

**"The dog wagged it's tail because its a good boy."**

Use the "it is" trick to spot the mistakes!

visualProps.text: The dog wagged it's tail because its a good boy.
visualProps.label: Spot the punctuation issue:

screens.title: Here's the fix — and the magic trick!
screens.body:
Holly swapped it's and its — used the apostrophe for possession and left it out for the contraction. This is THE most common mistake in English — even professional writers get it wrong!

**Rule:** 'its tail' = the tail belonging to the dog (possessive, no apostrophe). 'it's a good boy' = it is a good boy (contraction).

**The replacement test (works every time!):**
1. Find every "its" or "it's" in your writing
2. Replace each one with "it is" and read aloud
3. Does "it is" make sense? → use **it's** (with apostrophe)
4. Does "it is" NOT make sense? → use **its** (no apostrophe)

steps.text: WRONG: "The dog wagged it's tail because its a good boy."
steps.why: swapped it's and its — used the apostrophe for possession and left it out for the contraction

steps.text: RIGHT: "The dog wagged its tail because it's a good boy."
steps.why: 'its tail' = the tail belonging to the dog (possessive, no apostrophe). 'it's a good boy' = it is a good boy (contraction)

steps.text: Always use the replacement trick: swap in 'it is'
steps.why: If it makes sense → it's. If not → its. Simple and foolproof!

screens.title: Fix this sentence!
screens.body:
Which version is correct?

**"The school painted it's walls. It's playground was also improved."**

interaction.question: Which is correct?
options: The school painted its walls. Its playground was also improved.
The school painted it's walls. It's playground was also improved.
The school painted it's walls. Its playground was also improved.
The school painted its walls. It's playground was also improved.
The school painted its' walls. Its' playground was also improved.
interaction.correctAnswer: The school painted its walls. Its playground was also improved.
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Both are possessive — 'the walls belonging to the school' and 'the playground belonging to the school'. No apostrophe needed. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! The correct version is: **"The school painted its walls. Its playground was also improved."**. Both are possessive — 'the walls belonging to the school' and 'the playground belonging to the school'. No apostrophe needed. Always try replacing with 'it is' to check!

screens.title: it's vs its — you'll never mix them up again!
screens.body: You've now got the trick that works every single time. This alone could save you marks in the exam:

steps.text: Step 1: Replace the word with 'it is'
steps.why: Read the sentence with 'it is' and listen carefully

steps.text: Step 2: Does 'it is' make sense?
steps.why: If YES → write it's (with apostrophe). Easy!

steps.text: Step 3: Does 'it is' sound wrong?
steps.why: If NO → write its (no apostrophe). Done!

steps.text: Example: 'The cat licked ___ paw'
steps.why: 'The cat licked it is paw' — that sounds bonkers! So it's **its** (no apostrophe). You've got this! ✓

learningGoal: You'll discover parenthetical commas (commas in pairs that wrap around extra information) — they're like two hands cupping a bonus detail in the middle of a sentence
A handy trick: if you can lift the words out and the sentence still makes sense, your commas are in the right place!

screens.title: Did you know commas can work in pairs?
screens.body:
Aisha is writing a report about her school for the local newspaper. Look at this sentence:

**"Our headteacher, Mrs Green, is very kind."**

See those two commas around **"Mrs Green"**? They're like a pair of brackets — they wrap around a bonus fact that you could take out and the sentence would still work!

visualProps.text: Our headteacher, Mrs Green, is very kind.
visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: The brilliant removal test!
screens.body: In Aisha's sentence, **"Mrs Green"** is wrapped in commas. Parenthetical commas always come in **pairs** — like bookends! They wrap around extra information that you could remove without breaking the sentence. Here's the clever test:

steps.text: Start: "Our headteacher, Mrs Green, is very kind."
steps.why: Find the words sitting between the two commas

steps.text: Extra information: "Mrs Green"
steps.why: This is a bonus detail — nice to know, but the sentence doesn't need it to work

steps.text: Remove it: "Our headteacher is very kind."
steps.why: Still makes perfect sense! That proves the commas are in the right place!

steps.text: Parenthetical commas ALWAYS come in pairs
steps.why: One before and one after — like bookends. Never use just one!

interaction.incorrectFeedback: So close! The extra information is **"Ella"**. Remove it and you get: "My best friend won the science prize." — the sentence still works perfectly. Try the removal test next time!

visualProps.text: My best friend, Ella, won the science prize.

screens.title: Paired commas — you've cracked the code!
screens.body: Remember: commas for extra information always come in PAIRS — like bookends! Using these correctly makes your writing look really sophisticated:

steps.text: Parenthetical commas wrap around extra information
steps.why: Like bookends or brackets — one before, one after. They always travel together!

steps.text: Use the removal test: take out the words between the commas
steps.why: If the sentence still makes sense, your commas are in the right place

steps.text: ALWAYS use a pair — never just one comma!
steps.why: Forgetting the second comma is the most common mistake — now you won't make it!

steps.text: Examples: 'My sister, who is ten, loves reading.'
steps.why: Remove 'who is ten' — 'My sister loves reading.' Still works! That's the proof! ✓

learningGoal: You'll spot when parenthetical commas (paired commas around extra information) have gone missing or wandered off
The golden rule: these commas always travel in pairs — lose one and the whole sentence gets confusing!

screens.title: One comma is missing its partner!

**Rule:** Parenthetical commas come in PAIRS — you need one before AND one after the extra information.

steps.text: WRONG: "Queen Victoria, who ruled for 63 years was very powerful."
steps.why: forgot the second comma after the extra information 'who ruled for 63 years'

steps.text: RIGHT: "Queen Victoria, who ruled for 63 years, was very powerful."
steps.why: Parenthetical commas come in PAIRS — you need one before AND one after the extra information

steps.text: Parenthetical commas always come in PAIRS
steps.why: One before the extra info, one after — like bookends. They're lost without each other!

screens.title: Paired commas — sorted!

steps.text: Find any extra information in your sentences

learningGoal: You'll see how one tiny comma can completely flip what a sentence means — prepare for some funny examples!
Why popping a comma after an opening word or phrase stops your reader from getting totally confused

screens.title: The comma that saved Grandma's life!
screens.body:

**"Let's eat, Grandma!"**

visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: One comma prevents a LOT of confusion!
screens.body: Remember **"Let's eat Grandma!"** vs **"Let's eat, Grandma!"**? When a sentence starts with a time or place phrase, you need a comma after it. Without one, the meaning can get hilariously wrong!

steps.text: CONFUSING: "After eating the dog went for a walk."
steps.why: Without the comma, it sounds like someone ate the dog!

steps.text: CLEAR: "After eating, the dog went for a walk."
steps.why: The comma separates the opener from the main sentence

steps.text: Rule: put a comma after time/place openers
steps.why: After eating, ... / While painting, ... / Before washing, ...

steps.text: Also use commas when talking TO someone
steps.why: "Let's eat, Grandma!" — the comma shows you're addressing a person

learningGoal: You'll unlock the three superpowers of the ellipsis (the three dots ...): making a voice trail off, building suspense, and showing words have been left out
The strict rule that surprises people — an ellipsis is always exactly THREE dots, no more, no less!

screens.title: The mysterious three dots...
screens.body:
Ella is writing a mystery story for the school competition. Ella keeps seeing these three dots everywhere:

**"She opened the door slowly and saw..."**

Those three dots are called an **ellipsis** (say: eh-LIP-sis). They're one of the coolest punctuation marks — and they do some really clever things in writing!

visualProps.text: I think I might have... oh, never mind.
visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: Three dots, three superpowers!
screens.body: In Ella's sentence **"She opened the door slowly and saw..."**, the three dots (...) are called an **ellipsis**. It's a powerful little mark — and it has three main jobs:

interaction.sentence: An ellipsis is always exactly ____ dots
options: two
three
four
as many as you like
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Exactly three dots (...) — no more, no fewer. You've got it! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! An ellipsis must be exactly THREE dots (...). Two dots or four dots? That's just wrong! Remember: three, always three.

steps.text: 1. Trailing off: "I think I might have... oh, never mind."
steps.why: The character trails off mid-sentence — they stop themselves from finishing

steps.text: 2. Building suspense: "She opened the door slowly and saw..."
steps.why: The three dots build suspense — the reader has to wait to find out what she saw!

steps.text: 3. Missing words: "To be or not to be... that is the question."
steps.why: The ellipsis shows some of Shakespeare's original words have been left out

steps.text: Always use exactly THREE dots
steps.why: Two dots or four dots are incorrect — it must be three (...)

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
What is the ellipsis doing in this sentence?

interaction.question: What is the ellipsis used for here?
options: It is building suspense
It is showing missing words
It is showing a list continues
It is replacing a full stop
It is a spelling mistake
interaction.correctAnswer: It is building suspense
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! The ellipsis here is building suspense. Can you feel that pause and wonder? That's the power of three dots! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: So close! The ellipsis in "The house was silent. Then, from the attic..." is actually building suspense. Read it aloud and you can feel the dramatic pause it creates!

visualProps.text: The house was silent. Then, from the attic...
visualProps.label: Check the punctuation:

screens.title: Ellipsis — three dots, three jobs, sorted!
screens.body: Remember the three dots (...) and their three superpowers. Using these well will make your creative writing seriously impressive:

steps.text: Trailing off — when someone stops mid-sentence
steps.why: 'I was going to... oh, never mind.' — you can almost hear them change their mind!

steps.text: Building suspense — creating a cliffhanger or tension
steps.why: 'She opened the box and found...' — the reader HAS to keep reading!

steps.text: Missing words — showing words have been left out
steps.why: 'To be or not to be... that is the question.' — neat and tidy

steps.text: ALWAYS exactly three dots: ...
steps.why: Two dots (..) or four dots (....) are always wrong. Three is the magic number! ✓

learningGoal: You'll catch ellipsis (three dots ...) mistakes that pop up all the time — even in published books!
The two big blunders: using the wrong number of dots, or sticking an ellipsis where a different punctuation mark should go

**Rule:** An ellipsis is always three dots (...), never two or four.

steps.why: used four dots instead of three — an ellipsis is always exactly three dots

steps.text: RIGHT: The cave was dark and cold... Something moved in the shadows.
steps.why: An ellipsis is always three dots (...), never two or four

steps.text: Three dots. Always three. Exactly three.
steps.why: Count them on your fingers if you need to! It's worth getting right.

screens.title: Ellipsis — you've mastered the three dots!
screens.body: Before you use an ellipsis, run through this checklist. Used well, it makes your writing seriously dramatic:

steps.text: Always use exactly three dots: ...
steps.why: Not two, not four, not six — three is the magic number!

steps.text: Use it for trailing off, suspense, or missing words
steps.why: Three uses for three dots — easy to remember!

steps.text: Don't overuse it — once or twice per paragraph is plenty
steps.why: Too many ellipses make writing feel broken. Less is more!

steps.text: An ellipsis is a real punctuation mark — treat it with respect!
steps.why: Used well, it creates mystery and drama. That's the kind of writing that impresses examiners! ✓

learningGoal: You'll discover when two describing words need a hyphen to team up — like 'well-known' or 'ice-cold' — these are called compound adjectives (two words joined to describe something)
How hyphens act like tiny bridges joining words together in numbers, prefixes (word beginnings like 're-' or 'pre-'), and compound words

screens.title: The tiny line with a big job!
screens.body:
Aisha is describing characters in her creative writing. Did you know a tiny little line can change how words work together? Look at the difference:

**"a well-known author"** (with hyphen)
**"the author is well known"** (no hyphen)

The hyphen (-) joins two words together when they come BEFORE the noun they describe. It's like a bridge between them!

visualProps.text: "a well-known author" vs "the author is well known"
visualProps.label: With or without a hyphen?

screens.title: Hyphens — the word-joining superpower!
screens.body: We saw **"a well-known author"** (with hyphen) and **"the author is well known"** (without). Hyphens are like tiny bridges that join words together. Here are the rules:

interaction.sentence: A compound adjective needs a hyphen when it comes ____ the noun
options: before
after
anywhere near
instead of
interaction.correctFeedback: Exactly! "A well-known author" needs the hyphen because "well-known" comes BEFORE the noun. You've got this! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Compound adjectives need a hyphen when they come BEFORE the noun, like "a well-known author". Before = hyphen!

steps.text: Compound adjectives BEFORE a noun: "a well-known author"
steps.why: Before the noun, the two words act as ONE adjective, so they need a hyphen to join them

steps.text: Numbers: "twenty-one"
steps.why: Numbers from twenty-one to ninety-nine are always hyphenated

steps.text: Prefixes: "pre-Victorian"
steps.why: Prefixes before proper nouns need a hyphen: pre-Victorian, post-Brexit

steps.text: NO hyphen AFTER the noun: "the author is well known"
steps.why: After the noun, no hyphen is needed — the words stand on their own

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct version is: **"a well-known singer"**. 'well-known' comes before the noun 'singer', so it needs a hyphen to join the two words into one adjective

screens.title: Hyphens — tiny but mighty!
screens.body: Hyphens are small but knowing when to use them makes your writing look really polished. Here's your guide:

steps.text: Compound adjective BEFORE a noun → hyphen
steps.why: 'a well-known author' (but 'the author is well known') — position is everything!

steps.text: Numbers twenty-one to ninety-nine → always hyphenated
steps.why: thirty-five, forty-two, sixty-seven — no exceptions!

steps.text: Prefixes before proper nouns or dates → hyphen
steps.why: pre-Victorian, post-2020, non-fiction — the hyphen keeps things clear

steps.text: Compound adjective AFTER a noun → no hyphen
steps.why: 'The student is hard working' — no hyphen needed when it comes after. Knowing this is a real flex! ✓

learningGoal: You'll spot where hyphens have gone missing or sneaked into the wrong place
The common hyphen traps in compound adjectives (joined describing words) and numbers — and how to sidestep them

screens.title: The hyphens have gone missing!

**Rule:** Compound adjectives before nouns and numbers from twenty-one to ninety-nine always need hyphens.

steps.text: WRONG: "The well known detective solved the mystery in twenty one days."

steps.why: Compound adjectives before nouns and numbers from twenty-one to ninety-nine always need hyphens

steps.text: Hyphens join words that work together

screens.title: Hyphens — you've cracked the code!
screens.body: Check your writing for missing hyphens. Most people don't bother — but getting them right makes your writing look brilliant:

steps.text: Compound adjective before a noun? → add a hyphen
steps.why: well-known, old-fashioned, hard-working — they're a team!

steps.text: A number from twenty-one to ninety-nine? → add a hyphen
steps.why: thirty-five, forty-two, sixty-seven — no exceptions!

steps.text: A prefix like non-, self-, ex-, pre-, post-? → usually needs a hyphen
steps.why: non-fiction, self-confident, ex-champion, pre-Victorian

steps.text: After the noun? → no hyphen needed
steps.why: 'The author is well known' — no hyphen here. Position is everything! ✓

learningGoal: You'll learn the dash's secret talent — adding emphasis, afterthoughts, and dramatic surprise to your writing
The dash (—) vs the hyphen (-): they look similar but do completely different jobs — you'll never mix them up again!

screens.title: Meet the most dramatic punctuation mark!
screens.body:
Charlie is studying how authors create dramatic sentences. Look at how dashes create drama:

**"She opened the box and found — nothing."**

The dash (—) is the show-off of the punctuation world! It's DIFFERENT from a hyphen (-) and it creates **emphasis**, **surprise**, or a **dramatic pause**. Think of it as punctuation with attitude!

visualProps.text: The answer — believe it or not — was zero.
visualProps.label: Read the sentence:

screens.title: Three ways dashes make your writing amazing!
screens.body: Look at Charlie's sentence: **"She opened the box and found — nothing."**. Dashes (—) are like the special effects of punctuation — they've got three main jobs:

steps.text: 1. Emphasis in pairs: "The answer — believe it or not — was zero."
steps.why: The dashes add dramatic emphasis — like a spoken aside. They're stronger than commas

steps.text: 2. Dramatic surprise: "She opened the box and found — nothing."
steps.why: The single dash creates a dramatic pause before the surprise ending

steps.text: 3. Vivid description: "The old house — dark, cold and empty — stood at the end of the lane."
steps.why: The dashes work like brackets, adding a vivid description with extra drama

steps.text: Dashes (—) are NOT hyphens (-)
steps.why: A hyphen joins words (ice-cream). A dash adds drama! They look similar but do completely different jobs.

It is showing missing words like an ellipsis
interaction.correctAnswer: It is introducing a dramatic surprise
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The dash here is introducing a dramatic surprise. Can you feel the drama? That's the power of a dash! ✓

screens.title: Dashes — your secret dramatic weapon!
screens.body: Dashes are like the special effects of punctuation — they add drama and flair! Using them well makes your creative writing really stand out:

steps.text: A pair of dashes for emphasis: 'The dog — a huge Great Dane — bounded over.'
steps.why: Like brackets or commas, but with way more drama!

steps.text: A single dash for surprise: 'She opened the door and saw — nothing.'
steps.why: Creates a pause before the unexpected ending — makes your reader gasp!

steps.text: Dashes (—) are NOT hyphens (-)
steps.why: Hyphens join words. Dashes add drama. They look similar but are totally different!

steps.text: Use dashes sparingly for maximum impact
steps.why: One or two per paragraph keeps them powerful — too many and they lose their magic ✓

learningGoal: You'll catch the moment a dash and a hyphen have been swapped — it happens more often than you'd think!
You'll spot sentences where a dash has been used incorrectly and know exactly how to fix them

screens.title: Dash disaster — can you spot it?
screens.body:
Zara is comparing dashes and hyphens in a punctuation quiz. Something's gone wrong with the dashes in this sentence — can you figure out what?

**"The castle - ancient and crumbling - was said to be haunted."**

visualProps.text: The castle - ancient and crumbling - was said to be haunted.
visualProps.label: Spot the punctuation issue:

screens.title: Here's the fix!
screens.body:
Zara's mistake: **used hyphens (-) instead of dashes (—) for emphasis**. Dashes and hyphens look so similar that mixing them up is really common — but now you know the difference!

Hyphens (-) join words together (like 'well-known'). Dashes (—) add emphasis or interruption to a sentence.

steps.text: WRONG: "The castle - ancient and crumbling - was said to be haunted."
steps.why: used hyphens (-) instead of dashes (—) for emphasis

steps.text: RIGHT: "The castle — ancient and crumbling — was said to be haunted."
steps.why: Hyphens (-) join words together (like 'well-known'). Dashes (—) add emphasis or interruption to a sentence

steps.text: Remember: a dash (—) is NOT a hyphen (-)
steps.why: Dashes add drama. Hyphens join words. They look similar but do completely different jobs!

screens.body:
Which version is correct?

**"The test - believe it or not - was actually easy."**

interaction.question: Which version is correct?
options: The test — believe it or not — was actually easy.
The test - believe it or not - was actually easy.
The test - believe it or not - was actually easy.
The test - believe it or not - was actually easy.
The test ; believe it or not ; was actually easy.
interaction.correctAnswer: The test — believe it or not — was actually easy.
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! This needs dashes (—) not hyphens (-), because 'believe it or not' is an emphatic interruption, not a compound word ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct version is: **"The test — believe it or not — was actually easy."**. This needs dashes (—) not hyphens (-), because 'believe it or not' is an emphatic interruption, not a compound word

screens.title: Dashes — you're a pro now!
screens.body: Dashes are powerful — but only when used well! Now you know the rules, you can use them like a real author:

steps.text: A dash (—) is NOT a hyphen (-)
steps.why: Dashes add drama. Hyphens join words like 'well-known'. Knowing the difference is impressive!

steps.text: Dashes need spaces around them
steps.why: 'found — a puppy' NOT 'found—a puppy' — give them room to breathe!

steps.text: Use dashes sparingly — one or two per paragraph
steps.why: Too many dashes and they lose their power. Less is more!

steps.text: Dashes add drama: emphasis, surprise, or interruption
steps.why: They're the strongest way to create a dramatic moment. Use them well and your writing will really stand out ✓
